title: Bentonite detox cold-process soap
subtitle: A clay bar with olive oil, coconut oil, and activated bentonite at 5% superfat
excerpt: Bentonite clay gives this cold-process bar a smooth, silky feel on the skin and a pale warm-grey colour. The clay is dispersed in a portion of the oil before adding to the batter at trace, which prevents clumping and keeps the bar smooth throughout the mould. A 500 g oil batch produces 8 firm bars after the 6-week cure.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Bentonite clay in soap: Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), clay additions chapter. Clay-in-oil dispersion method to prevent clumping: Bramble Berry soapmaking reference library (2023).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by recalculating lye weight for the new oil total using a lye calculator. Do not adjust the lye figure by hand.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: 48 hours in the mould before unmoulding; 4 to 6 weeks on a curing rack before use.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place on an open rack. Discard if bars develop orange oily specks (DOS) or a sharp rancid smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of the oil weight left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5% superfat, 5% of the oil stays as free conditioning oil.
The moment when the soap batter thickens to a warm-custard consistency and a drizzle on the surface holds briefly before sinking. The signal to pour into the mould.

content.text: Bentonite clay adds a silky, slip-on-skin quality to a cold-process bar and produces a pale warm-grey colour. The clay in this bar is dispersed in a tablespoon of oil before being stirred into the batter at
; mixing it directly into the batter causes clumps. The base is 67% olive oil, 28% coconut oil, and 5% castor oil, which gives a conditioning lather with good hardness and stable bubbles. At 5%

and 33% lye discount, the bar is mild and suitable for daily use. Yield is 8 bars of around 125 g each after cure. The scent is left unscented in this formulation; add up to 12 g of a chosen essential oil at trace if wanted.

items.prepNote: 28% of oil weight; melted if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat
Used for the lye solution; chilled to room temperature before use
3% of oil weight; dispersed in 1 tablespoon of oil before adding at trace

content.text: Put on protective gloves and goggles. Work in a ventilated space. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide and add it to the water in a slow, steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. The solution heats sharply; set aside to cool to 38 to 42°C.

Scoop 1 tablespoon of olive oil from the weighed batch into a small cup. Stir in the bentonite clay until you have a smooth, lump-free paste. Set aside. This step prevents the clay from clumping when it meets the full batter.
Weigh the olive oil (minus the tablespoon used for clay) and castor oil into a stainless-steel bowl. Melt the coconut oil and weigh into the same bowl. Cool the oil blend to 38 to 42°C.
Pour the lye solution into the oils in a slow stream. Blend with the stick blender in short 5-second bursts until the batter reaches light

. Scrape in the clay-oil paste and blend for 5 more seconds until fully incorporated. Pour immediately into the silicone loaf mould, tap the mould once to settle the batter, and wrap in a towel. Leave undisturbed for 48 hours.

Unmould after 48 hours. Wear gloves; the bar is caustic until fully cured. Cut into bars using the wire cutter or a sharp knife. Place on a curing rack with space between each bar and leave for 4 to 6 weeks, turning every 2 weeks.

Store cured bars on an open rack or soap dish in a cool, dry place. Full shelf life is two years. Discard any bar that develops orange oily spots (DOS, rancid superfat oil) or smells sharp and chemical.
